title-group.article-title: Current treatment strategies for targeting virulence factors and biofilm formation in Acinetobacter baumannii

contrib-group.aff: 1Department of Genetic Engineering, School of Bioengineering, College of Engineering & Technology, SRM Institute of Science & Technology, Kattankulathur, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, 603203, India

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 Expert Publishing Medicine Ltd trading as Taylor & Francis

abstract.p: A higher prevalence of Acinetobacter baumannii infections and mortality rate has been reported recently in hospital-acquired infections (HAI). The biofilm-forming capability of A. baumannii makes it an extremely dangerous pathogen, especially in device-associated hospital-acquired infections (DA-HAI), thereby it resists the penetration of antibiotics. Further, the transmission of the SARS-CoV-2 virus was exacerbated in DA-HAI during the epidemic. This review specifically examines the complex interconnections between several components and genes that play a role in the biofilm formation and the development of infections. The current review provides insights into innovative treatments and therapeutic approaches to combat A. baumannii biofilm-related infections, thereby ultimately improving patient outcomes and reducing the burden of HAI.

abstract.title: Plain language summary
abstract.p: Acinetobacter baumannii is a type of bacteria that spreads quickly in the hospital environment. It is extremely dangerous, as it can form protective communities on the surface of medical devices, known as a biofilm. Biofilms can affect the ability of antibiotics to kill the bacteria. This review looks at how A. baumannii forms biofilms and ways that biofilms can be disrupted to kill the bacteria.

sec.title: Acinetobacter baumannii, prime pathogen in device-associated infection

list-item.p: The most endemic bacterial pathogens from hospital units causing nosocomial infections have been documented with multidrug-resistant profiles such as Enterococcus faecium, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Enterobacter spp. (ESKAPE).
Among the ESKAPE group A. baumannii is an important Gram-negative coccobacillus pathogenic bacteria causing nosocomial infections in the respiratory and urinary tracts.

Several investigations of infectious diseases have alluded that there is a connection between the biofilm and the pathogenesis of A. baumannii. 70% of all bacterial infections are mediated by the biofilm and are mainly device associated.

sec.title: Virulence factors associated with adhesion & biofilm formation

list-item.p: The outer membrane protein A plays a crucial role in bacterial adherence, biofilm formation, infection in host cells, antibiotic resistance, surface motility and immunomodulation.
A. baumannii has Bfms sensor kinase that acts as a two-component system (TCS), and its major functions include biofilm formation control, cell adhesion, pellicle formation and serum killing.
PNAG is the predominant polysaccharide present in the extracellular matrix of A. baumannii biofilm. It is synthesized by the Pga machinery comprised of PgaA, PgaB, PgaC and PgaD proteins.
A new molecule that is GNAT-family acetyltransferase has been involved in biofilm formation and it is conserved in A. baumannii.

A. baumannii utilizes a peculiar mechanism to scavenge the iron from host cells through siderophores. A. baumannii is reported to produce three important siderophores named Acinetobactin, baumannoferrin and fimsbactin. Among the three siderophores, acinetobactin is a major influence in the pathogenesis and virulence as it possesses a high-affinity catechol hydroxamate siderophore that competes with the host cell for iron.
Carbohydrate-binding proteins like lectin have been studied for their ability to inhibit the adhesion of A. baumannii.
The structural integrity of the biofilm can be disrupted by these enzymes which makes the bacteria within the biofilm more susceptible to treatment.
AMP LL-37 has demonstrated antimicrobial activity, and antibiofilm activity against A. baumannii it was reported to modulate the host immune response, which may aid in bacterial clearance.

Phage therapy has various advantages including minimal impact on the microbiome; phages can replicate within the infected bacteria, amplifying their therapeutic effect; safe and well tolerated, with minimal side effects observed in clinical trials.
Polymer-based anti-adherent can be coated on the medical device surfaces to avoid the colonization of pathogens on the device surfaces.

The fabrication of a potential nanocarrier loaded with an anti-microbial agent or biofilm inhibitor molecule and tagged with an anti-oxidant will serve as a potential alternative therapeutic approach.
A two-recombinant-pilus proteins vaccine that targets the CsuA/B protein and a similar fimbriae protein, FimA, was shown to protect mice from getting infected with A. baumannii.

Surface modifications of medical devices could be a promising prevention approach that is developing antimicrobial or anti-biofilm coatings for medical devices and hospital surfaces could reduce A. baumannii transmission.
Future healthcare strategies should emphasize rigorous infection control protocols and training to minimize A. baumannii spread within healthcare facilities.
The One Health approach will also be a potential approach to investigating environmental reservoirs of A. baumannii.
Nano vaccines and nano-encapsulated AMP will be a promising approach in the future theranostic.
Comparative genomics, subtractive proteomics, and interactome analyses have been used to improve the shortlisted vaccine candidates in addition to bioinformatics tools with simple outputs.
Acetylation of 1,3 DAP is important for abiotic surface adhesion and biofilm production. Hence, Dpa will be a potential drug target against the drug-resistant and biofilm-forming A. baumannii.
